text: The main difference between the EU and the US approaches to the legal regime of personal data is

options.text: that the EU treats personal data as an aspect of individual personality, whereas the US treats data as a market
that the EU treats personal data as an aspect of individual personality and dignity, whereas the US treats data as a market and relates to liberty
that only the EU protects privacy
that only the US protects privacy
that only the US approach leverages individual consent to protect privacy

text: Correcting illegal bias in AI

options.text: is always legitimate
must be done in a way that does not violate basic legal principles such as equality
is legally impossible
can be done only by amending the algorithm or the dataset for the training

text: The processing of personal data pursuant to the GDPR may be lawfully carried out

options.text: When data subjects expressed their own consent
Based on the controller's free choice
When there is no consent by data subjects, but the processing is needed for protecting the data subjects' or other individuals' vital interests
When there is no consent, but the processing must take place to perform a contract between the controller and any third party

text: Which of the following is/are legislative instruments belonging to EU primary law?

options.text: Treaty of the European Union, Treaty on the functioning of the European Union, Case-law of the European Court of Justice
Treaty of the European Union, Treaty on the functioning of the European Union, Charter of fundamental rights of the European Union
Charter of fundamental rights of the European Union, Regulations, Case-law of the European Court of Justice

text: Which of the following answers is/are correct?

options.text: (1) Primary Law, (2) Secondary Law, (3) International Agreements
(1) Founding treaties, (2) International Agreements, (3) Secondary Law
(1) Primary Law, (2) Secondary Law, (3) Member States law

text: Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.text: Freedom of thought cannot be affected by AI technologies
Freedom of thought is not considered as a human right in most jurisdictions
Freedom of thought deserves protection only once the individual shares his thoughts with others
Freedom of thought is considered as a human right in most jurisdictions but hardly protected in itself

text: Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.text: EU regulations and directives must be directly applied in any of their provisions in all Member States
Regulations are directly applicable in all Member States as such, whereas directives need to be implemented by every Member State
Directives are directly applicable in all Member States as such, whereas regulations need to be implemented by every Member State
Regulations and directives are adopted by the EU legislator

text: When the European Court of Human Rights rules that a State has failed to protect a right of an individual:

options.text: The Court's ruling replaces the domestic rule that is incompatible with the European Convention of Human Rights
It is up to the State to remove the violation of the European Convention
The individual can sue the State in the European Court of Human Rights

text: The European Data Protection Supervisor is:

options.text: A national authority supervising on data protection
A supranational authority supervising on the activity of national supervisory authorities
A supranational supervisor on any processing of personal data of Member States citizens
An independent body at the European level supervising on processing carried out by EU Institutions

text: The European Data Protection Supervisor:

options.text: gathers the European Data Protection Board and the National Supervisory Authorities
is in charge of handling any report of personal data breach committed by any entity within the EU
is responsible for handling individuals' reports of data breaches committed by EU institutions only

text: The European Data Protection Board is:

options.text: An institution provided for by the Treaty on the European Union
An agency of the European Commission with the aim of protecting the fundamental right to data protection
An independent body gathering the national supervisory authorities of each EU member State
Vested with the power to decide over cases involving EU staff members in their personal data protection processing

text: The Charter of fundamental rights recognizes the right to privacy and the right to data protection to:

options.text: Only individuals with EU citizenship
All individuals provided that they are in the EU
Only EU companies

text: What is the difference between regulations and directives in EU law?

options.text: Directives must be applied directly in all EU Member States as well as Regulations
Regulations are immediately enforceable, while directives need domestic execution
Regulations must be directly applied in every country, whereas Directives must be implemented by EU Member States to become applicable
Regulations are binding, while directives are only exhortations
Regulations establish rules, whereas directives introduce principles
Regulations, as well as Directives, must be implemented by every Member State in order to be fully applicable
There is no difference between the two notions

text: Social Credit Systems are:

options.text: Intrinsically incompatible with basic legal principles
Problematic insofar as they are opaque and have wide ramifications for the legal, economic, and social life of a subject
Forbidden under Chinese law
Forbidden under U.S. Law

text: The EU Charter of fundamental rights expressly safeguards

options.text: The right of data controllers and processors to process anyone's personal data
The right of individuals to personal data protection
The right of individuals to private and family life
The right of individuals to process any other individuals' personal data

text: Should data controllers and data processors be separate entities, the GDPR sets out that:

options.text: data controllers are totally free to indicate one or more data processors, the latter not being bound by any obligation towards data controllers
their relationships need to be regulated by specific contractual agreements or by different acts provided for by law
their mutual relationships need to be regulated only by an order of any competent National Supervisory Authority

text: What is the difference between synthetic and anonymized data?

options.text: Anonymized data are covered by the GDPR, whereas synthetic data are not
Anonymized data lose most details and are therefore less useful to train algorithms
Synthetic data are covered by the GDPR, whereas anonymized data are not
Only anonymization can be reversed to reidentify individuals

text: Why is the protection of personal data insufficient to protect fundamental rights?

options.text: Because it only covers specific countries; there is no macroregional standardized regulation
Internet users cannot cope with massive consent requests when they visit websites
Because AI doesn't use personal data
Because AI can be biased

text: The E-Privacy Directive:

options.text: provides for personal data protection in processing activities carried out by EU institutions
needed to be implemented in every Member State
was directly applicable in every Member State since its entry into force
regulates personal data processing in connection with public communications services in public communications networks within the EU

text: The European Electronic Communications Code (so-called Recast Directive):

options.text: deals with personal data processing activities in the communication industry
aims at ensuring equal and fair access to electronic communications services, promoting at the same time connectivity across the EU
does not concern personal data processing activities

text: What is the legal treatment of synthetic data?

options.text: Synthetic data are protected by the GDPR.
Synthetic data are not covered by the GDPR.
Anonymized data are protected by the GDPR.
Synthetic data are prohibited under the GDPR.
Synthetic data and anonymized data are the same notion.

text: Why is the protection of personal data so important?

options.text: Because the EU does not protect synthetic data
The protection of personal data usually serves to protect people from manipulation and exploitation
Because AI does not need personal data
Because AI can be biased.

text: A Data Protection Impact Assessment under the GDPR is:

options.text: compulsory only when the appointment of a DPO is required by law
compulsory only in specific cases expressly set out by law
mandatory for any kind of processing activities involving non-personal data
compulsory for every kind of processing activities involving personal data

text: A data controller is:

options.text: discretionally entitled to reject a data subject's request to access their personal data
always required to grant access to a data subject's personal data by anyone requesting it
under an obligation to grant access to personal data concerning a specific data subject requesting it, unless otherwise set out by law
required to grant access to personal data to subjects other than the data subject, when set forth by law
required to grant access to personal data only when data subjects submitted a related request also to the data processor

text: The AI Act is:

options.text: A soft law legal instrument offering guidelines for using AI
A hard law regulation adopted by the EU
An international convention governing the use of AI worldwide
An act adopted by the EU which third countries will be obliged to adopt
A regulation adopted by the EU which might influence extra-EU countries in adopting similar legislation

text: The Brussels Effect means that:

options.text: All economic operators coming from outside the EU are bound to comply with EU law
Many third countries will be legally required to adopt the same legislation adopted by Belgium
Some extra-EU legal systems adopted, or will likely adopt, legislation similar to those first adopted by the EU
Some third countries will conclude international conventions with the EU for applying EU legislation

text: Which of the following statements is/are false?

options.text: The GDPR applies only to EU citizens within the EU
The GDPR applies to any data subject within the EU
The GDPR applies to data controllers incorporated in third countries when they operate in the EU
The GDPR does not apply to data controllers incorporated in third countries when they process personal data belonging to EU citizens

text: The 2014 judgment issued by the European Court of Justice in case Google Spain (C-131/12):

options.text: paved the way to recognize the right to personal data protection in the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights
paved the way to include the right to be forgotten in the GDPR as one of the rights recognized to the data subject's benefit
originated from preliminary ruling proceedings referred to the ECJ by a Spanish court
paved the way to include the right to be forgotten in the GDPR as one of the rights recognized to the data controller's benefit

text: Which is the correct order of the following sources of EU law in the EU-related hierarchy (from the top to the bottom)?

options.text: Regulations/Directives, International Agreements, Charter of Fundamental Rights, Decisions
Founding Treaties/Charter of Fundamental Rights, Regulations/Directives
Founding Treaties/Charter of Fundamental Rights, International Agreements, Regulations/Directives
